---
title: "Two Theorems That Make Statistics Possible: LLN and the CLT"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240607)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # samples / histograms
LIMIT  = "#c1121f"     # the limiting object (truth, normal curve)
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / exact values
GREY   = "#6c757d"

def normal_pdf(t, mu=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    """The Gaussian density — used only to overlay the CLT's prediction."""
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article in the **Foundations of
> Estimation** movement, and it is the theoretical hinge the whole track swings on. It
> assumes [INFR-A1 *The Estimator as a Random Variable*], which established that a statistic
> like the sample mean is itself a random variable with a **sampling distribution**, and
> that its standard deviation — the **standard error** — measures how much the estimate
> wobbles from sample to sample. INFR-A1 left two promissory notes open: *why does the
> wobble shrink as we collect more data*, and *what shape does the wobble take*. This
> article pays both. It borrows three results, each built from scratch elsewhere and
> imported here only by name: **Chebyshev's inequality** [PROB-19], the
> **moment-generating / characteristic function** and its uniqueness and Taylor properties
> [PROB-20], and the **modes of convergence** — convergence in probability and in
> distribution — together with **Lévy's continuity theorem** and **Slutsky's theorem**
> [PROB-21]. It **sets up** the rest of the track: the CLT-based confidence interval
> [INFR-C1], the asymptotic normality of the maximum-likelihood estimator [INFR-B4], and the
> null distributions of the workhorse test statistics [INFR-D2]. Everything downstream that
> says "approximately normal" or "the standard error is $\sigma/\sqrt n$" traces back to the
> two theorems proved here.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

All of frequentist statistics rests on two limit theorems, and this article proves both
with nothing above a Calculus-I floor. The **Law of Large Numbers** says the sample mean of
$n$ independent measurements converges to the true mean as $n$ grows — *averaging more data
homes in on the truth*. We prove the weak form in three lines from Chebyshev's inequality
and read off the rate for free: the probability of a miss shrinks like $1/n$. The **Central
Limit Theorem** says that once you subtract that true mean and magnify the leftover wobble
by exactly $\sqrt n$, the distribution of the result settles onto a single universal shape —
the Gaussian — *no matter what distribution the data came from*. We prove it by following
the moment-generating function through the limit and watching every distribution's
fingerprint collapse to the same bell curve, and we derive *why the magnification factor is
$\sqrt n$ and not $n$ or $\sqrt[3]{n}$*. The single claim the article defends is that these
two theorems are what make a sample mean *usable*: the LLN says it is eventually right, the
CLT says exactly how uncertain it is along the way. We close at the honest edge — a
heavy-tailed sample where the magnification is too slow to trust, and a Cauchy sample where
the mean is the wrong target and both theorems fail outright — so the reader leaves knowing
not just the theorems but their boundary.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Let $X_1, X_2, \dots$ be independent draws from a distribution with mean $\mu$ and finite
variance $\sigma^2$, and let $\bar X_n = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ be the sample mean.

**Law of Large Numbers (weak form).** The sample mean converges *in probability* to the
truth:
$$
\bar X_n \xrightarrow{\;P\;} \mu
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
P\bigl(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\bigr)\to 0
\ \text{ for every } \varepsilon>0.
$$
*More data makes the average reliably close to the true mean.*

**Central Limit Theorem.** The *re-centered, re-scaled* sample mean converges *in
distribution* to a standard Gaussian:
$$
\frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}
\;=\;
\frac{\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n - \mu)}{\sigma}
\xrightarrow{\;d\;}
\mathcal N(0,1).
$$
*Whatever the data's distribution, the estimation error is approximately Gaussian, and its
typical size is $\sigma/\sqrt n$.*

The LLN tells you the estimate is eventually **right**; the CLT tells you, along the way,
exactly **how wrong** it is likely to be — and that the answer wears the same bell-shaped
suit no matter what you sampled.
:::

Everything orbits that box. The LLN is the promise that estimation is even possible — that
collecting data is not futile. The CLT is the promise that we can put a number on our
remaining ignorance, the *standard error* $\sigma/\sqrt n$, and that the number means the
same thing (a Gaussian spread) across wildly different problems. The two together are why a
single piece of machinery — *estimate $\pm$ a multiple of the standard error* — works for
the mean of incomes, the proportion of voters, the average lifetime of a part, and a
thousand other things that share no other family resemblance.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the theorems {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the whole article: **why is a sample mean trustworthy, and
how trustworthy is it?** INFR-A1 made the danger vivid. A sample mean is a *random
variable*: collect a different sample and you get a different number. So when a pollster
reports "$52\%$ support," that $52$ is one draw from a distribution of possible answers. Two
worries follow immediately, and a working statistic must answer both.

1. **Does the noise wash out?** If I keep collecting data, does my estimate settle down
   toward the real answer, or could it wander forever? Without a guarantee here, more data
   is not obviously better, and statistics has no foundation.
2. **How big is the remaining error, and what does it look like?** Even granting that the
   estimate settles, at any finite sample size there is a gap between estimate and truth. To
   report honest uncertainty — a margin of error, a confidence interval — I need the
   *distribution* of that gap, not just a vague sense that it is "small."

The first worry is answered by the **Law of Large Numbers**, the second by the **Central
Limit Theorem**. They are not abstract ornaments; they are the two load-bearing beams under
every standard error, every confidence interval, and every p-value in the rest of this
track.

## A little history, because it carries the why {#sec-history}

The LLN is the older idea. **Jacob Bernoulli**, in *Ars Conjectandi* (published
posthumously in 1713), proved the first version for coin-flip proportions and called it his
"golden theorem." His motivation was exactly worry (1): he wanted to justify *learning a
probability from its observed frequency* — that if you flip a biased coin enough times, the
observed fraction of heads will, with near-certainty, sit as close as you please to the true
bias. Bernoulli grasped that this needed *proof*, not assumption; it is the mathematical
license for the entire empirical enterprise of estimating an unknown chance from data.

The CLT took longer and answers worry (2). **Abraham de Moivre** (1733) found that the
binomial distribution, suitably scaled, was approximated by the bell curve — the first
central limit result, for coin flips only. **Pierre-Simon Laplace** generalized it, and
**Carl Friedrich Gauss** tied the same curve to measurement error (which is why we call it
*Gaussian*). But the clean modern statement — *any* distribution with finite variance,
scaled by $\sqrt n$, becomes Gaussian — was nailed down by **Aleksandr Lyapunov** (1901)
and **Jarl Lindeberg** (1922), using exactly the characteristic-function machinery we will
borrow. The name "central limit theorem" is **George Pólya's** (1920): *central* not because
it is in the middle of anything, but because it is *central to the subject* — the theorem
the whole field is organized around.

That history is the *why* in one sentence: **Bernoulli made estimation possible; de
Moivre–Laplace–Gauss made its uncertainty quantifiable.** Keep that split in mind — it is
exactly the LLN/CLT split of the Core — and every step below will feel like an answer to one
of the two original worries rather than an arbitrary manipulation.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one breath, what is the difference between the LLN and the CLT?"* The LLN is a statement
about a **point**: $\bar X_n$ collapses onto the single number $\mu$. The CLT is a statement
about a **shape**: the *fluctuations of $\bar X_n$ around $\mu$*, blown up by $\sqrt n$,
spread out into a Gaussian. The LLN kills the variability ($\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)\to
0$); the CLT looks at that vanishing variability under a microscope tuned to exactly the
right magnification and finds universal structure in it. One says *where* the estimate goes;
the other says *how* it gets there.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any proof, two pictures.

**The LLN picture: a shrinking cloud.** Imagine running the experiment "average $n$ draws"
over and over for a fixed $n$, and plotting where $\bar X_n$ lands each time. You get a cloud
of points centered on $\mu$. INFR-A1 already told us this cloud has standard deviation
$\sigma/\sqrt n$ — the standard error. As $n$ grows, $\sigma/\sqrt n$ shrinks, so the cloud
tightens around $\mu$. The LLN is the statement that the cloud tightens *all the way to a
point*: for any window around $\mu$, however narrow, eventually essentially all of the cloud
is inside it. @fig-lln-funnel shows a single long run of $\bar X_n$ as $n$ climbs, funneling
in on the truth.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lln-funnel
#| fig-cap: 'The Law of Large Numbers as a funnel. For each of five independent experiments we draw a long stream from an Exponential distribution with true mean $\mu=2$ (a skewed, decidedly non-normal source) and plot the running average $\bar X_n$ against $n$ on a log axis. Every path is jittery at small $n$ but is squeezed toward the true mean (red line) as $n$ grows. The grey band is $\mu \pm 2\sigma/\sqrt n$, the $\pm 2$ standard-error envelope from INFR-A1; the paths stay inside it about as often as the CLT will later predict, and the band itself narrows like $1/\sqrt n$.'
mu_true = 2.0
sigma_true = 2.0           # Exponential(rate 1/2): mean = sd = 2
N = 4000
ns = np.arange(1, N + 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for k in range(5):
    draws = rng.exponential(scale=mu_true, size=N)
    running = np.cumsum(draws) / ns
    ax.plot(ns, running, color=DATA, alpha=0.55, lw=1.0)
ax.axhline(mu_true, color=LIMIT, lw=2.0, label=r"true mean $\mu=2$")
ax.fill_between(ns, mu_true - 2 * sigma_true / np.sqrt(ns),
                mu_true + 2 * sigma_true / np.sqrt(ns),
                color=GREY, alpha=0.22, label=r"$\mu \pm 2\sigma/\sqrt{n}$")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$ (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"running average $\bar X_n$")
ax.set_ylim(0.5, 3.5)
ax.set_title("Five running averages funnel onto the truth")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**The CLT picture: a shape that forgets where it came from.** Now stop letting the cloud
collapse. Instead, after each averaging, *re-magnify* the leftover gap $\bar X_n - \mu$ by
$\sqrt n$ — just enough to undo the shrinking — and histogram the magnified gaps. The
astonishing fact is that this histogram converges to a fixed bell curve, and the bell curve
is the *same* whether the raw draws were coin flips, exponential waiting times, or dice
rolls. The lumpiness of the source distribution gets ironed out by averaging.
@fig-clt-emergence shows the bell curve emerging from a wildly non-normal source as $n$
grows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-clt-emergence
#| fig-cap: 'The Central Limit Theorem in action, from a source that looks nothing like a bell. The raw data are draws from a *discrete* distribution on $\{0,1,10\}$ with probabilities $(0.6, 0.3, 0.1)$ — lumpy and right-skewed (top-left). We form the standardized sample mean $Z_n=\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n-\mu)/\sigma$ and histogram $30{,}000$ independent values of it for $n=2,10,50$. The histograms march toward the standard normal density (red), forgetting the three spikes they came from. The convergence is in *distribution*: the shape settles, even though the underlying draws never become continuous.'
vals = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 10.0])
probs = np.array([0.6, 0.3, 0.1])
mu = np.sum(vals * probs)
var = np.sum(probs * (vals - mu) ** 2)
sigma = np.sqrt(var)

def standardized_means(n, reps=30000):
    draws = rng.choice(vals, size=(reps, n), p=probs)
    means = draws.mean(axis=1)
    return np.sqrt(n) * (means - mu) / sigma

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8.6, 6.2))
grid = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)

axes[0, 0].bar(vals, probs, width=0.5, color=DATA)
axes[0, 0].set_title("Raw source: spikes at 0, 1, 10")
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel("value"); axes[0, 0].set_ylabel("probability")
axes[0, 0].grid(False)

for ax, n in zip([axes[0, 1], axes[1, 0], axes[1, 1]], [2, 10, 50]):
    z = standardized_means(n)
    ax.hist(z, bins=60, density=True, color=DATA, alpha=0.75,
            range=(-4, 4))
    ax.plot(grid, normal_pdf(grid), color=LIMIT, lw=2.0)
    ax.set_title(fr"standardized mean, $n={n}$")
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$Z_n$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Notice already, in the top-right panel of @fig-clt-emergence, that $n=2$ is *not* yet
Gaussian — the skew of the source still shows. That lag is the honest subject of
@sec-finite-sample. The theorem is a statement about the *limit*; how fast a real, finite
sample approaches it depends on how ugly the source is.

# The Law of Large Numbers {#sec-lln}

We prove the weak law first, because it is short, exact, and hands us the convergence rate
for free. It rests entirely on one imported inequality.

## The one imported tool: Chebyshev {#sec-cheby}

::: {#thm-chebyshev}
**Chebyshev's inequality (imported from [PROB-19]).** Let $Y$ be any random variable with
finite mean $E[Y]$ and finite variance $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$. Then for every $\varepsilon>0$,
$$
P\bigl(|Y - E[Y]| \ge \varepsilon\bigr) \;\le\; \frac{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}{\varepsilon^2}.
$$
:::

This is the single result we borrow for the LLN, and [PROB-19] derives it from scratch (it
is two lines from Markov's inequality applied to $(Y-E[Y])^2$). We do not re-prove it; we
*flag* it as imported and use its statement. In words: a random variable rarely lands many
"variance-widths" from its mean, and the probability of a large miss is bounded by the
variance divided by the square of the miss size — *no knowledge of $Y$'s shape required*.
That distribution-freeness is exactly why it works for the sample mean of an *arbitrary*
source.

## Two facts about the sample mean we need {#sec-mean-moments}

To apply Chebyshev to $Y = \bar X_n$ we need its mean and variance. Both were established in
INFR-A1, but they are short enough to re-derive here so nothing is implicit, using only the
linearity of expectation and the variance-of-a-sum rule for independent variables (both
built in the Probability track, [PROB-05] and [PROB-08]).

Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be independent, each with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, and write
$\bar X_n = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$.

**Its mean.** Expectation is linear — $E[aX+bY]=aE[X]+bE[Y]$ — so the expectation of a sum is
the sum of expectations and constants pull out:
$$
E[\bar X_n]
= E\!\left[\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right]
= \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n E[X_i]
= \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \mu
= \frac1n\,(n\mu)
= \mu.
$$ {#eq-mean-of-mean}

So $\bar X_n$ is **unbiased**: on average it equals the truth. This is half of why averaging
is sensible — it does not systematically over- or under-shoot.

**Its variance.** Two rules. First, scaling a random variable by a constant $c$ multiplies
its variance by $c^2$, because variance is built from squared deviations:
$\operatorname{Var}(cZ)=E[(cZ-cE[Z])^2]=c^2E[(Z-E[Z])^2]=c^2\operatorname{Var}(Z)$. Second,
for **independent** variables the variance of a sum is the sum of the variances (the
cross-terms $E[(X_i-\mu)(X_j-\mu)]$ vanish for $i\ne j$ under independence — derived in
[PROB-08]). Apply both, with $c=1/n$:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2)
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
$$ {#eq-var-of-mean}

There it is — the fact the whole article hangs on. **The variance of the sample mean is
$\sigma^2/n$, so its standard deviation, the standard error, is $\sigma/\sqrt n$.** It
shrinks to zero as $n\to\infty$. The $\sqrt n$ in the denominator (from the square root of
the $n$ in @eq-var-of-mean) is the same $\sqrt n$ that will reappear as the CLT's
magnification factor; that is no coincidence, and we will see why in @sec-why-root-n.

## The weak law, proved {#sec-wlln-proof}

::: {#thm-wlln}
**Weak Law of Large Numbers.** Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be independent random variables, each with
the same mean $\mu$ and the same finite variance $\sigma^2$. Then the sample mean converges
to $\mu$ *in probability*: for every $\varepsilon>0$,
$$
P\bigl(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\bigr)\;\longrightarrow\;0
\qquad\text{as } n\to\infty.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The entire proof is: *apply Chebyshev to $\bar X_n$.* We have everything we need —
@eq-mean-of-mean says $E[\bar X_n]=\mu$, and @eq-var-of-mean says
$\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)=\sigma^2/n$. Substitute $Y=\bar X_n$ into @thm-chebyshev. Its
mean $E[Y]$ is $\mu$, so the event $\{|Y-E[Y]|\ge\varepsilon\}$ is exactly
$\{|\bar X_n-\mu|\ge\varepsilon\}$, and its variance is $\sigma^2/n$:
$$
P\bigl(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\bigr)
\;\le\; \frac{\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)}{\varepsilon^2}
\;=\; \frac{\sigma^2/n}{\varepsilon^2}
\;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n\,\varepsilon^2}.
$$ {#eq-wlln-bound}

Now fix $\varepsilon>0$ and let $n\to\infty$. The right-hand side $\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n\varepsilon^2}$
has $\sigma^2$ and $\varepsilon^2$ fixed and $n$ in the denominator, so it is a constant over
$n$ and tends to $0$. The left-hand side is a probability, so it is squeezed between $0$ and
that vanishing bound:
$$
0 \;\le\; P\bigl(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\bigr) \;\le\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n\,\varepsilon^2}
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\; 0.
$$
By the squeeze, the middle term tends to $0$. Since $\varepsilon>0$ was arbitrary, this is
exactly the definition of convergence in probability ([PROB-21]): $\bar X_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Three lines, and worry (1) from @sec-why is settled: **average enough independent
measurements and the average is, with overwhelming probability, as close to the truth as you
like.** Two observations make this proof worth more than its length.

**First — the rate is free.** The bound @eq-wlln-bound is not just qualitative. To force the
miss probability below a target $\delta$, set $\sigma^2/(n\varepsilon^2)\le\delta$ and solve:
$$
n \;\ge\; \frac{\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^2\,\delta}.
$$ {#eq-lln-rate}
So to be within $\varepsilon$ of the truth with probability $1-\delta$, it *suffices* to take
$n$ at least $\sigma^2/(\varepsilon^2\delta)$ samples. This is a genuine, if conservative,
sample-size rule that falls straight out of the proof. (It is conservative precisely because
Chebyshev is conservative — @sec-finite-sample and the CLT give a much sharper count when the
source is well-behaved.)

**Second — what we did and did not assume.** We assumed a finite variance $\sigma^2$ and
independence. We did *not* assume any particular distribution: not normal, not symmetric, not
continuous. The Exponential paths in @fig-lln-funnel and the three-spike source in
@fig-clt-emergence both obey this law for the same reason. That universality is the point.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between the weak law and the strong law?"* The **weak** law (what we
proved) says $\bar X_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu$: for each fixed large $n$, the probability that
*this* $\bar X_n$ is far from $\mu$ is small. The **strong** law says
$\bar X_n\xrightarrow{a.s.}\mu$ — *almost-sure* convergence — that with probability one the
*entire sequence* $\bar X_1,\bar X_2,\dots$ settles down and never wanders far again. The
strong law is genuinely stronger (almost-sure convergence implies convergence in probability;
[PROB-21] proves the hierarchy and that it does not reverse), and its standard proofs need
more machinery — fourth moments, or the Borel–Cantelli lemma, or Kolmogorov's inequality —
which is why the weak law, provable in three lines from Chebyshev, is the one we lean on. For
the working statistician the practical content is the same: more data, better estimate. The
distinction matters when you reason about a *single long run* of an algorithm (strong law)
versus a *fixed sample size repeated* (weak law).
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The LLN does not say later draws "compensate" for earlier ones — that is the gambler's
fallacy.** After a run of ten heads, the LLN does *not* push the coin toward tails to "balance
out." The draws are independent; the coin has no memory. What actually happens is *dilution*,
not compensation: the early imbalance becomes a smaller and smaller fraction of an
ever-larger total. Ten extra heads out of $20$ flips is a $50\%$ surplus; ten extra heads out
of $20{,}000$ flips is a $0.05\%$ surplus. The sum of deviations need not shrink — in fact it
typically grows like $\sqrt n$, which is precisely the CLT — but *divided by $n$* it vanishes.
The average converges because the denominator outruns the numerator, not because the future
corrects the past.
:::

## Sanity-checking the rate against the bound {#sec-lln-numeric}

The bound @eq-wlln-bound is exact arithmetic, so we can watch a simulated miss probability sit
under it. @fig-lln-bound does this for the three-spike source of @fig-clt-emergence.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lln-bound
#| fig-cap: 'Chebyshev''s bound on the LLN, checked by simulation. For the three-spike source ($\mu, \sigma^2$ as computed in @fig-clt-emergence) and a fixed tolerance $\varepsilon=0.5$, the blue dots are the simulated $P(|\bar X_n-\mu|\ge\varepsilon)$ over $20{,}000$ replicates at each $n$; the red curve is the Chebyshev bound $\sigma^2/(n\varepsilon^2)$. The bound always sits above the truth (it must — it is a theorem) and both march to zero like $1/n$. The gap is the price of distribution-freeness: Chebyshev knows only the variance, so it cannot exploit the source''s actual shape.'
vals = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 10.0])
probs = np.array([0.6, 0.3, 0.1])
mu = np.sum(vals * probs)
var = np.sum(probs * (vals - mu) ** 2)
eps = 0.5

n_grid = np.array([5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, 640])
emp = []
for n in n_grid:
    draws = rng.choice(vals, size=(20000, n), p=probs)
    means = draws.mean(axis=1)
    emp.append(np.mean(np.abs(means - mu) >= eps))
emp = np.array(emp)
bound = var / (n_grid * eps ** 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(n_grid, bound, color=LIMIT, marker="o", label=r"Chebyshev bound $\sigma^2/(n\varepsilon^2)$")
ax.plot(n_grid, emp, color=DATA, marker="s", label=r"simulated $P(|\bar X_n-\mu|\geq\varepsilon)$")
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$ (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"miss probability (log scale)")
ax.set_title(r"The bound dominates the truth, both $\sim 1/n$")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The bound is loose — often by an order of magnitude — but it is *valid for every $n$* and
needs nothing but the variance. That is the recurring trade we will see again: distribution-
free guarantees (Chebyshev, the LLN) are robust but conservative; distributional results (the
CLT, next) are sharp but lean on an assumption.

# The Central Limit Theorem {#sec-clt}

The LLN crushed the variability of $\bar X_n$ to zero. The CLT puts that vanishing
variability under a microscope and finds a universal shape. We first settle the question the
LLN already poses — *why magnify by $\sqrt n$?* — then state and prove the theorem.

## Why the magnification is exactly $\sqrt n$ {#sec-why-root-n}

The CLT studies the gap $\bar X_n - \mu$. By the LLN this gap collapses to $0$, so to see any
structure we must blow it up by some factor $a_n\to\infty$ and ask: which $a_n$ gives a
*non-degenerate* limit — neither exploding to infinity nor collapsing to a spike at $0$? The
answer is forced by matching the variance, and we can find it before proving anything.

Consider the scaled gap $a_n(\bar X_n-\mu)$. Its mean is $a_n\cdot 0 = 0$ (using
@eq-mean-of-mean). Its variance, using $\operatorname{Var}(cZ)=c^2\operatorname{Var}(Z)$ and
@eq-var-of-mean, is
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\bigl(a_n(\bar X_n-\mu)\bigr)
= a_n^2\,\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= a_n^2\cdot\frac{\sigma^2}{n}
= \sigma^2\,\frac{a_n^2}{n}.
$$
For the limit to be non-degenerate, this variance must approach a *finite, nonzero* constant
— if it went to $0$ the scaled gap would collapse to a spike at $0$ (we would not have
magnified enough), and if it went to $\infty$ the distribution would spread out forever (too
much magnification). A finite nonzero limit requires $a_n^2/n$ to settle on a constant, i.e.
$a_n^2\propto n$, i.e.
$$
a_n \propto \sqrt n.
$$
Choosing $a_n=\sqrt n$ exactly makes the variance equal to $\sigma^2$:
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\bigl(\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n-\mu)\bigr) = \sigma^2 \quad\text{for every } n.
$$ {#eq-root-n-var}
So $\sqrt n$ is the *unique* magnification (up to a constant) that keeps the spread fixed as
$n$ grows. Dividing additionally by $\sigma$ standardizes the variance to $1$, giving the
quantity in the Core,
$$
Z_n \;=\; \frac{\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n - \mu)}{\sigma}, \qquad E[Z_n]=0,\quad \operatorname{Var}(Z_n)=1.
$$ {#eq-Zn}
The CLT will say $Z_n\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$. Matching the variance, as we just did,
pins down the *scaling* and the *mean and variance* of the limit; what it cannot tell us is
the *shape*. That the shape is Gaussian — and the same Gaussian regardless of the source — is
the content of the theorem, and it needs the moment-generating function to prove.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why $\sqrt n$ and not $n$?"* Because the standard error scales like $1/\sqrt n$
(@eq-var-of-mean), so to undo the shrinking and hold the spread fixed you must multiply by
$\sqrt n$. Multiply by the full $n$ and you over-correct: the variance becomes
$\sigma^2 n\to\infty$ and the distribution blows apart. Multiply by less and you under-correct
and get a spike. The $\sqrt n$ is the Goldilocks rate, and it is *the same* $\sqrt n$ that
shows up in the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ and in the $\sqrt n$ growth of the *sum*'s
fluctuations — three faces of the one $1/n$ in the variance of the mean.
:::

## The second imported tool: the moment-generating function {#sec-mgf}

For the LLN, Chebyshev was enough. For the CLT we need a tool that controls *shape*, not just
spread, and that turns the sum inside $\bar X_n$ into something manageable. That tool is the
**moment-generating function (MGF)**, built in full in [PROB-20]. We import three facts.

::: {#def-mgf}
**Definition (MGF, imported from [PROB-20]).** The moment-generating function of a random
variable $X$ is $M_X(t)=E\!\left[e^{tX}\right]$, defined for $t$ in an open interval around
$0$ where the expectation is finite.
:::

The three imported properties, each derived from scratch in [PROB-20], are these.

**(P1) Taylor expansion in terms of moments ([PROB-20], @thm-moments there).** Where it
exists, the MGF has the expansion about $t=0$
$$
M_X(t) = 1 + E[X]\,t + \frac{E[X^2]}{2}\,t^2 + \frac{E[X^3]}{6}\,t^3 + \cdots,
$$
because $e^{tX}=\sum_k (tX)^k/k!$ and expectation passes through the series term by term, so
the coefficient of $t^k$ is the $k$-th moment $E[X^k]/k!$. We need only the first three terms.

**(P2) The MGF of a sum of independent variables is the product of their MGFs ([PROB-20]).**
If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, $e^{t(X+Y)}=e^{tX}e^{tY}$, and independence lets the
expectation of the product split into the product of expectations:
$M_{X+Y}(t)=E[e^{tX}e^{tY}]=E[e^{tX}]E[e^{tY}]=M_X(t)M_Y(t)$. This is the property that makes
the MGF the natural tool for a *sum* — and the sample mean is a scaled sum.

**(P3) Uniqueness and continuity (the convergence bridge).** If the MGFs $M_{Z_n}(t)$
converge, for every $t$ in a neighborhood of $0$, to the MGF $M_Z(t)$ of a random variable
$Z$, then $Z_n\xrightarrow{d}Z$ — convergence of MGFs implies convergence in distribution.
This is the moment-generating-function form of **Lévy's continuity theorem** ([PROB-21],
@thm-levy, stated there for characteristic functions); it is the rigorous bridge from "the
transforms match in the limit" to "the distributions match in the limit," and rests on the
**uniqueness theorem** that an MGF determines its distribution ([PROB-20], @thm-uniqueness).

::: {.callout-note title="A note on rigor: MGF versus characteristic function"}
The cleanest fully general proof of the CLT uses the **characteristic function**
$\varphi_X(t)=E[e^{itX}]$ instead of the MGF, because $\varphi_X$ exists for *every*
distribution with finite variance, whereas $M_X$ can fail to exist (heavy-tailed laws have no
MGF). The two arguments are *identical line for line* — replace $t$ by $it$ throughout — and
[PROB-20] builds both. We run the proof with the MGF because the algebra reads more cleanly to
a rusty eye and the logic is unchanged; the characteristic-function version, which removes the
"assume the MGF exists" caveat, is the one a measure-theory course would insist on. We flag
this honestly rather than hide it: the *theorem* needs only finite variance; our *proof*, as
written, additionally assumes the MGF exists near $0$, and the cf-version lifts that crutch.
:::

## The theorem, and its proof {#sec-clt-proof}

::: {#thm-clt}
**Central Limit Theorem (i.i.d. case).** Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be independent and identically
distributed with mean $\mu$ and finite, nonzero variance $\sigma^2$. Then
$$
Z_n \;=\; \frac{\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n - \mu)}{\sigma}
\;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{X_i-\mu}{\sigma}
\;\xrightarrow{\;d\;}\; \mathcal N(0,1).
$$
:::

Before the proof, confirm the two forms of $Z_n$ agree. Starting from the middle expression
and writing $\bar X_n=\frac1n\sum X_i$:
$$
\frac{\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n-\mu)}{\sigma}
= \frac{\sqrt n}{\sigma}\left(\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i - \mu\right)
= \frac{\sqrt n}{\sigma}\cdot\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)
= \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)
= \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{X_i-\mu}{\sigma},
$$
using $\sqrt n/n = 1/\sqrt n$ and distributing $\mu = \frac1n\sum\mu$ inside the sum. The
right-hand form shows $Z_n$ as a *normalized sum of i.i.d. standardized pieces*, which is
exactly the shape the MGF was built to handle.

::: {.proof}
**Step 0 — standardize the summands.** Define
$$
W_i = \frac{X_i - \mu}{\sigma}.
$$
The $W_i$ are i.i.d. (a fixed shift and scale of i.i.d. variables), and they are
*standardized*: by linearity of expectation $E[W_i]=\frac{E[X_i]-\mu}{\sigma}=\frac{\mu-\mu}
{\sigma}=0$, and by the scaling rule for variance
$\operatorname{Var}(W_i)=\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2}=1$.
Because $E[W_i]=0$, the second moment equals the variance: $E[W_i^2]=\operatorname{Var}(W_i)+
(E[W_i])^2 = 1 + 0 = 1$. In this notation $Z_n=\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n W_i$.

**Step 1 — the MGF of one standardized summand.** Let $m(t)=M_{W}(t)=E[e^{tW}]$ be the common
MGF of the $W_i$ (assumed to exist near $0$; see the note above). By property (P1), its Taylor
expansion about $0$ uses the moments we just computed, $E[W]=0$ and $E[W^2]=1$:
$$
m(t) = 1 + \underbrace{E[W]}_{=0}\,t + \frac{\overbrace{E[W^2]}^{=1}}{2}\,t^2 + r(t)
     = 1 + \frac{t^2}{2} + r(t),
$$ {#eq-m-expand}
where $r(t)$ collects the cubic-and-higher terms. The key fact about $r(t)$, which is all we
use, is that it is *negligible compared to $t^2$ near $0$*: $r(t)/t^2\to 0$ as $t\to 0$. (This
is just the statement that a Taylor remainder past the quadratic term is smaller order than
the quadratic; it requires $E[W^2]<\infty$, which we have, and is made precise in [PROB-20]'s
treatment of the expansion.)

**Step 2 — the MGF of $Z_n$.** Here properties (P2) and the scaling of the MGF combine. First,
the MGF of a scaled variable $cW$ is $M_{cW}(t)=E[e^{t(cW)}]=E[e^{(ct)W}]=m(ct)$ — scaling the
variable rescales the argument. Each summand of $Z_n$ is $W_i/\sqrt n$, i.e. $c=1/\sqrt n$, so
each contributes $m(t/\sqrt n)$. Since the $W_i$ are independent, (P2) turns the MGF of their
sum into a product, and because they are *identically* distributed all $n$ factors are equal:
$$
M_{Z_n}(t)
= M_{\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum_i W_i}(t)
= \prod_{i=1}^n M_{W_i/\sqrt n}(t)
= \prod_{i=1}^n m\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)
= \left[\,m\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)\right]^{n}.
$$ {#eq-MZn}

**Step 3 — expand the inside.** Substitute $t/\sqrt n$ for the argument in @eq-m-expand. Since
$(t/\sqrt n)^2 = t^2/n$,
$$
m\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)
= 1 + \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{t^2}{n} + r\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)
= 1 + \frac{1}{n}\!\left[\frac{t^2}{2} + n\,r\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)\right].
$$
Call the bracket $c_n(t)=\frac{t^2}{2}+n\,r\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)$, so that
$$
M_{Z_n}(t) = \left[\,1 + \frac{c_n(t)}{n}\,\right]^{n}.
$$ {#eq-MZn-cn}

**Step 4 — the remainder washes out.** Fix $t$. As $n\to\infty$, the argument $t/\sqrt n\to 0$,
so we may use the negligibility of $r$ from Step 1. Write the remainder term as
$$
n\,r\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)
= \underbrace{\frac{r(t/\sqrt n)}{(t/\sqrt n)^2}}_{\to\, 0}\cdot\, n\cdot\frac{t^2}{n}
= \frac{r(t/\sqrt n)}{(t/\sqrt n)^2}\cdot t^2.
$$
The middle step just multiplied and divided by $(t/\sqrt n)^2 = t^2/n$ and used $n\cdot t^2/n
= t^2$. As $n\to\infty$ the fraction $\to 0$ (Step 1) while $t^2$ is fixed, so $n\,r(t/\sqrt
n)\to 0$. Therefore
$$
c_n(t) = \frac{t^2}{2} + n\,r\!\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt n}\right) \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{t^2}{2}.
$$ {#eq-cn-limit}

**Step 5 — the famous limit.** We need $\lim_{n\to\infty}\bigl[1+c_n(t)/n\bigr]^n$ where
$c_n(t)\to c=t^2/2$. This is the compound-interest limit. Recall the Calc-1 fact, taken as
known at our floor,
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(1 + \frac{c}{n}\right)^n = e^{c}.
$$ {#eq-e-limit}
We need the version where the constant $c$ is replaced by a sequence $c_n\to c$; the limit is
still $e^c$. Here is the one-line justification so it is not assumed. Take logarithms:
$\ln\bigl[(1+c_n/n)^n\bigr] = n\ln(1+c_n/n)$. For large $n$ the argument $c_n/n\to 0$, and the
Taylor expansion of the logarithm at $1$ is $\ln(1+u)=u-\tfrac{u^2}{2}+\cdots$, so
$$
n\ln\!\left(1+\frac{c_n}{n}\right)
= n\left[\frac{c_n}{n} - \frac{1}{2}\Bigl(\frac{c_n}{n}\Bigr)^2 + \cdots\right]
= c_n - \frac{c_n^2}{2n} + \cdots
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\; c,
$$
because $c_n\to c$ and every later term carries a $1/n$ (or higher) that kills it. Exponentiating
the limit of the logarithm (the exponential is continuous, so it passes through the limit)
gives $e^{c}$. Applying this with $c=t^2/2$:
$$
M_{Z_n}(t) = \left[\,1+\frac{c_n(t)}{n}\,\right]^n \;\longrightarrow\; e^{\,t^2/2}.
$$ {#eq-MZn-limit}

**Step 6 — recognize the limit and conclude.** The function $e^{t^2/2}$ is exactly the MGF of
the standard normal $\mathcal N(0,1)$. (We can verify this directly: for $Z\sim\mathcal
N(0,1)$, $M_Z(t)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{tz}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}\,dz$; completing
the square in the exponent, $tz-\tfrac{z^2}{2}=-\tfrac12(z-t)^2+\tfrac{t^2}{2}$, pulls the
constant $e^{t^2/2}$ out front and leaves a standard normal density that integrates to $1$,
giving $M_Z(t)=e^{t^2/2}$. This computation is done in full in [PROB-13].) So
$M_{Z_n}(t)\to M_Z(t)$ for every $t$ near $0$, and by property (P3) — the continuity theorem —
convergence of MGFs implies convergence in distribution:
$$
Z_n \xrightarrow{\;d\;} \mathcal N(0,1). \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Step back and see what the proof *did*. The source distribution entered only through its
MGF $m(t)$, and only through the first three Taylor coefficients: the constant $1$, the mean
(which we zeroed by centering), and the second moment (which we set to $1$ by scaling).
*Everything else about the source — its skew, its kurtosis, its lumps and spikes — sits in the
remainder $r$, and Step 4 showed the remainder washes out under the $\sqrt n$ scaling.* That is
the mechanism of universality: the magnification by $\sqrt n$ is tuned so that only the
mean and variance survive into the limit, and all higher structure is squeezed to zero. The
bell curve is what is left when you keep two moments and erase the rest.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where exactly in the proof is the assumption of finite variance used, and what breaks
without it?"* In Step 1, the Taylor expansion @eq-m-expand keeps terms through $t^2$, whose
coefficient is $E[W^2]=1$ — *finite variance is what makes that coefficient exist and be a
real number*. If the variance is infinite (heavy tails), $m(t)$ has no quadratic term to
extract, the whole $\sqrt n$-scaling argument collapses, and the limit is generally *not*
Gaussian — it is a **stable law** with heavier tails, reached under a different scaling
$n^{1/\alpha}$ with $\alpha<2$. We meet exactly this failure in @sec-cauchy. Finite mean alone
gives you the LLN (well, the LLN needs finite variance too in our Chebyshev proof, though it
holds under just a finite mean by a harder argument); finite *variance* is the price of the
CLT.
:::

## Watching the proof's mechanism: the bell emerges {#sec-clt-numeric}

The proof says the bell appears because everything above the second moment is scrubbed away by
the $\sqrt n$ scaling. @fig-clt-sources makes that claim falsifiable: it runs the standardized
mean for three sources that disagree violently in shape but all have finite variance, and
overlays the *same* normal curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-clt-sources
#| fig-cap: "Universality. Three sources with finite variance but very different shapes — a fair coin (Bernoulli, two spikes), an Exponential (right-skewed, continuous), and a Uniform (flat) — each pushed through the standardized sample mean $Z_n$ at $n=30$, with $40{,}000$ replicates each. All three histograms converge to the *same* standard normal density (red). The sources are forgotten; only mean and variance survived. The skewed Exponential lags slightly (a touch of residual right-skew at $n=30$), foreshadowing @sec-finite-sample."
def standardized_mean_from(sampler, src_mu, src_sigma, n=30, reps=40000):
    draws = sampler(size=(reps, n))
    means = draws.mean(axis=1)
    return np.sqrt(n) * (means - src_mu) / src_sigma

sources = {
    "Bernoulli(0.5)": (lambda size: rng.integers(0, 2, size=size).astype(float), 0.5, 0.5),
    "Exponential(1)": (lambda size: rng.exponential(1.0, size=size), 1.0, 1.0),
    "Uniform(0,1)":   (lambda size: rng.uniform(0.0, 1.0, size=size), 0.5, np.sqrt(1/12)),
}

grid = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.6, 3.5), sharey=True)
for ax, (name, (samp, smu, ssig)) in zip(axes, sources.items()):
    z = standardized_mean_from(samp, smu, ssig)
    ax.hist(z, bins=55, density=True, range=(-4, 4), color=DATA, alpha=0.75)
    ax.plot(grid, normal_pdf(grid), color=LIMIT, lw=2.0)
    ax.set_title(name, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$Z_{30}$")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Why the CLT is what makes the standard error usable {#sec-usable}

This section is the *why* cashing out. INFR-A1 defined the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ as
the standard deviation of $\bar X_n$. By itself a standard deviation is just a spread number;
it does not tell you *how often* the estimate lands within one or two of them, because that
depends on the *shape* of the sampling distribution. The CLT supplies the shape — Gaussian —
and the Gaussian has famous, memorizable tail probabilities. That marriage is what turns a
spread into a usable statement of uncertainty.

Concretely, rearrange the CLT. Saying $Z_n=\frac{\bar X_n-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}$ is
approximately $\mathcal N(0,1)$ is the same as saying
$$
\bar X_n \;\approx\; \mathcal N\!\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
\qquad\text{for large } n.
$$ {#eq-clt-mean-form}
Now use the standard-normal fact (from [PROB-13]) that a $\mathcal N(0,1)$ variable lands in
$[-1.96, 1.96]$ with probability $0.95$. Translating through @eq-clt-mean-form, with
probability about $0.95$,
$$
-1.96 \le \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} \le 1.96
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\bar X_n - 1.96\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n} \;\le\; \mu \;\le\; \bar X_n + 1.96\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$ {#eq-ci-preview}
That interval $\bar X_n \pm 1.96\,\sigma/\sqrt n$ is the **confidence interval** for a mean —
the single most-used object in applied statistics — and it exists *only because the CLT tells
us the sampling distribution is Gaussian*. Without the CLT we would know the spread but not how
to convert it into a coverage statement. @fig-ci-coverage shows these intervals doing their
job. (We build this object carefully, including what "$95\%$ confident" does and does *not*
mean, in [INFR-C1]; here it is a preview of the payoff.)

```{python}
#| label: fig-ci-coverage
#| fig-cap: 'The CLT turning a standard error into coverage. From an Exponential source with true mean $\mu=2$ (red line) we draw $50$ samples of size $n=60$, and for each compute the CLT interval $\bar X_n \pm 1.96\,\sigma/\sqrt n$. Each horizontal bar is one interval; green bars cover the truth, red bars miss. About $95\%$ are expected to cover, and the count printed below confirms it. The intervals have a well-defined coverage rate *because* the CLT makes $\bar X_n$ approximately Gaussian — that is the whole point of the theorem for practice.'
mu_t, sig_t = 2.0, 2.0     # Exponential(mean 2): sd = 2
n = 60
reps = 50
covers = 0
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 5.0))
for j in range(reps):
    samp = rng.exponential(mu_t, size=n)
    xbar = samp.mean()
    half = 1.96 * sig_t / np.sqrt(n)
    lo, hi = xbar - half, xbar + half
    hit = lo <= mu_t <= hi
    covers += hit
    ax.plot([lo, hi], [j, j], color=(ACCENT if hit else LIMIT), lw=2.0)
    ax.plot(xbar, j, "o", color=DATA, ms=3)
ax.axvline(mu_t, color=LIMIT, lw=2.0, label=r"true $\mu=2$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\bar X_n \pm 1.96\,\sigma/\sqrt{n}$")
ax.set_ylabel("sample index")
ax.set_title(f"{covers}/{reps} intervals cover the truth")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"coverage: {covers}/{reps} = {covers/reps:.0%}  (target 95%)")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In practice you don't know $\sigma$ — you estimate it with $s$. Why is it still legitimate
to use the normal (or t) interval?"* Because of **Slutsky's theorem** ([PROB-21],
@thm-slutsky). The sample standard deviation $s$ converges in probability to $\sigma$ (a
constant) by the LLN applied to the squared deviations. Slutsky says that dividing a
sequence that converges in distribution (here $\sqrt n(\bar X_n-\mu)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal
N(0,\sigma^2)$) by a sequence converging in probability to a constant ($s\to\sigma$) leaves
the limiting distribution intact, only rescaled: $\frac{\sqrt n(\bar X_n-\mu)}{s}\xrightarrow
{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$. So plugging in the estimated $s$ is asymptotically free. The *small-sample*
correction for having estimated $\sigma$ is the **Student t-distribution**, the subject of
[INFR-C2]; the CLT plus Slutsky is why it converges back to the normal as $n$ grows.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-finite-sample}

The CLT is a statement about the *limit* $n\to\infty$. Every real sample is finite, so the
honest questions are: *how large must $n$ be for the Gaussian approximation to be good, and
when is it never good?* This is where most misuse happens, so we treat it carefully.

## The "$n\ge 30$" myth, dismantled {#sec-n30}

A rule repeated in countless intro courses says "the CLT kicks in at $n\ge 30$." This is
folklore, and it is wrong as a universal claim. **There is no magic $n$.** The sample size you
need depends entirely on how far the source is from Gaussian — specifically on its **skewness**
and **kurtosis** (its third and fourth standardized moments). A symmetric, light-tailed source
is nearly Gaussian after averaging just a handful of draws; a violently skewed or heavy-tailed
source can need hundreds or thousands. @fig-n30 makes this concrete by holding $n=30$ fixed and
varying only the source.

```{python}
#| label: fig-n30
#| fig-cap: 'The ''$n\geq 30$'' rule is not universal. All three panels use the *same* sample size $n=30$; only the source changes. **Left:** a symmetric Uniform source is essentially perfectly Gaussian after averaging — $n=30$ is overkill. **Middle:** a moderately skewed Exponential is close but visibly right-leaning. **Right:** a strongly skewed source (Exponential of an exponential — a log-normal-like heavy right tail) is still markedly non-Gaussian at $n=30$. The required $n$ is a property of the source''s skew, not a constant.'
grid = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)
n = 30
reps = 40000

def std_mean(sampler, smu, ssig, n=n, reps=reps):
    d = sampler((reps, n))
    m = d.mean(axis=1)
    return np.sqrt(n) * (m - smu) / ssig

# Uniform(0,1): mean 1/2, sd sqrt(1/12)
unif = (lambda s: rng.uniform(0, 1, s), 0.5, np.sqrt(1/12))
# Exponential(1): mean 1, sd 1
expo = (lambda s: rng.exponential(1.0, s), 1.0, 1.0)
# Strongly skewed: lognormal exp(N(0,1)) -> mean e^{0.5}, var (e-1)e
ln_mu = np.exp(0.5)
ln_sd = np.sqrt((np.exp(1) - 1) * np.exp(1))
logn = (lambda s: np.exp(rng.normal(0, 1, s)), ln_mu, ln_sd)

panels = [("Uniform (symmetric)", unif),
          ("Exponential (skew 2)", expo),
          ("Log-normal (skew ~6)", logn)]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.6, 3.5), sharey=True)
for ax, (name, (samp, smu, ssig)) in zip(axes, panels):
    z = std_mean(samp, smu, ssig)
    ax.hist(z, bins=60, density=True, range=(-4, 4), color=DATA, alpha=0.75)
    ax.plot(grid, normal_pdf(grid), color=LIMIT, lw=2.0)
    ax.set_title(f"{name}, $n=30$", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$Z_{30}$")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

There is even a quantitative version, the **Berry–Esseen theorem**, which we state but do not
prove (its proof is a substantial undertaking; we flag it as an imported fact). It says the
worst-case gap between the true distribution of $Z_n$ and the standard normal is bounded by
$$
\sup_z\bigl|\,P(Z_n\le z) - \Phi(z)\,\bigr| \;\le\; \frac{C\,\rho}{\sigma^3\sqrt n},
\qquad \rho = E\bigl[\,|X-\mu|^3\,\bigr],
$$
where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF and $C$ is a universal constant (around $0.41$). Read
it: the approximation error shrinks like $1/\sqrt n$ — *the same $\sqrt n$ again* — but the
numerator carries the **third absolute moment** $\rho$, which explodes for skewed, heavy-tailed
sources. A big $\rho$ means you need a big $n$ to drive the bound down. That is the honest,
quantitative replacement for the "$n\ge 30$" slogan: *the convergence rate is $1/\sqrt n$, with
a constant that depends on the source's third moment.*

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The CLT is about the distribution of the *average*, not the data.** A common error is to
look at a histogram of the raw data, see that it is not bell-shaped, and conclude "the CLT
doesn't apply." The CLT never claimed the data were Gaussian — it claims the *sample mean* (or
sum) is approximately Gaussian. Conversely, and more dangerously, people apply normal-theory
intervals to a *single* heavy-tailed measurement, or to a statistic that is not a sum or
average (a maximum, a ratio, a variance) without checking that a CLT-type result even holds for
it. The theorem is specifically about sums and averages of many independent, finite-variance
pieces. Outside that template, reach for the bootstrap ([INFR-E1]) instead of assuming
normality.
:::

## The edge of applicability: heavy tails and the Pareto mean {#sec-heavy}

Now the honest boundary the track demands. Consider a **Pareto** distribution — a power law,
the canonical model of incomes, city sizes, file sizes, and insurance losses. With shape
parameter $\alpha$, its tail falls like $P(X>x)\sim x^{-\alpha}$. The catch:

- The **mean** is finite only if $\alpha>1$.
- The **variance** is finite only if $\alpha>2$.

When $1<\alpha\le 2$, the mean exists but the variance is *infinite* — and **the CLT's
hypothesis (finite variance) fails**. The sample mean still converges to the true mean (the
LLN holds, since the mean is finite), but the *fluctuations* are not Gaussian and the
$\sqrt n$-standardization does not stabilize. @fig-pareto shows a Pareto with $\alpha=1.5$
(finite mean, infinite variance): the standardized sample mean refuses to become Gaussian even
at large $n$, dominated by occasional gigantic draws.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pareto
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability: a Pareto source with shape $\alpha=1.5$ — finite mean, *infinite* variance. Because the CLT''s finite-variance hypothesis fails, the standardized sample mean does **not** converge to a Gaussian. **Left:** even at $n=500$ the histogram of the (sample-SD-standardized) mean is sharply peaked with a long right tail, nothing like the normal curve (red). **Right:** the running average is repeatedly knocked off course by rare enormous draws and converges agonizingly slowly. The bell never forms; this is outside the CLT''s domain.'
alpha = 1.5
# scipy-free Pareto draws: inverse-CDF of Pareto(alpha) with xm=1 is U^{-1/alpha}
def pareto(size):
    u = rng.uniform(0, 1, size)
    return u ** (-1.0 / alpha)

true_mean = alpha / (alpha - 1.0)   # = 3.0 for alpha=1.5

# Left: standardized mean at n=500 using the SAMPLE sd (since true sd is infinite)
n = 500
reps = 20000
draws = pareto((reps, n))
means = draws.mean(axis=1)
sds = draws.std(axis=1, ddof=1)
z = np.sqrt(n) * (means - true_mean) / sds

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.7))
grid = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)
axL.hist(z, bins=80, density=True, range=(-4, 4), color=DATA, alpha=0.75)
axL.plot(grid, normal_pdf(grid), color=LIMIT, lw=2.0, label="normal (does NOT fit)")
axL.set_title(r"Pareto($\alpha=1.5$), $n=500$")
axL.set_xlabel(r"$Z_n$ (sample-SD standardized)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

ns = np.arange(1, 8001)
for _ in range(4):
    d = pareto(8000)
    axR.plot(ns, np.cumsum(d) / ns, color=DATA, alpha=0.6, lw=1.0)
axR.axhline(true_mean, color=LIMIT, lw=2.0, label=r"true mean $=3$")
axR.set_xscale("log")
axR.set_title("Running average: slow, jumpy")
axR.set_xlabel("$n$ (log scale)"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\bar X_n$")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The practical lesson is sharp: for heavy-tailed data (incomes, losses, network traffic), a
sample-mean-plus-CLT-interval can be badly miscalibrated even at large $n$, because the
infinite (or merely enormous) variance makes the $\sqrt n$ machinery either fail outright
($\alpha\le 2$) or converge far too slowly to trust. The fixes are real and named: work with a
**transformed** quantity (the log, where a log-normal becomes Gaussian-friendly), estimate a
**robust** center (the median or a trimmed mean, which have their own well-behaved CLTs),
report a **different functional** entirely, or get the sampling distribution by **bootstrap**
([INFR-E1]) — though even the bootstrap struggles in the infinite-variance regime, an honesty
[INFR-E1] makes explicit.

## When the mean is the wrong target at all: Cauchy {#sec-cauchy}

The hardest edge case is when even the *mean* fails to exist. The **Cauchy** distribution
(the bell-shaped but heavy-tailed law you get as the ratio of two independent standard
normals, or as $\tan$ of a uniform angle) has $\alpha=1$-style tails so heavy that $E[X]$ is
not defined — the defining integral $\int x\,f(x)\,dx$ does not converge. Here *both* theorems
fail, and spectacularly:

- The **LLN fails**: $\bar X_n$ does not converge to anything. In fact, a remarkable exact
  fact (provable in one line with characteristic functions, [PROB-20]) is that the average of
  $n$ i.i.d. Cauchy variables is *itself* Cauchy with the *same* spread — averaging buys you
  **nothing**. More data does not help at all.
- The **CLT fails**: there is no finite variance (indeed no finite mean) to standardize by.

@fig-cauchy shows the catastrophe: the running average of Cauchy draws wanders forever,
periodically yanked across the plane by a single colossal outlier, never settling.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cauchy
#| fig-cap: "Where both theorems die: the standard Cauchy distribution, which has no finite mean. **Left:** five running averages of Cauchy draws — they never settle, each jerked to a new level whenever a giant outlier arrives, in flat contradiction to the LLN funnel of @fig-lln-funnel. The 'true mean' (the distribution's center of symmetry, $0$, red) is never homed in on. **Right:** the *sample median* of the same draws, which **does** converge to $0$ — the right tool when the mean does not exist. The median has its own LLN and CLT; the mean simply has no target here."
def cauchy(size):
    return rng.standard_cauchy(size)

N = 4000
ns = np.arange(1, N + 1)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.7))
for _ in range(5):
    d = cauchy(N)
    axL.plot(ns, np.cumsum(d) / ns, color=DATA, alpha=0.6, lw=1.0)
axL.axhline(0.0, color=LIMIT, lw=2.0, label="center $=0$")
axL.set_xscale("log")
axL.set_ylim(-15, 15)
axL.set_title("Cauchy running MEAN never settles")
axL.set_xlabel("$n$ (log scale)"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\bar X_n$")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

for _ in range(5):
    d = cauchy(N)
    run_med = np.array([np.median(d[:k]) for k in ns])
    axR.plot(ns, run_med, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.6, lw=1.0)
axR.axhline(0.0, color=LIMIT, lw=2.0, label="center $=0$")
axR.set_xscale("log")
axR.set_ylim(-2, 2)
axR.set_title("Cauchy running MEDIAN converges")
axR.set_xlabel("$n$ (log scale)"); axR.set_ylabel("running median")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The right panel carries the constructive moral. When the mean is undefined or wild, *the mean
is the wrong summary*, and the fix is to estimate a different functional — here the **median**,
which exists for every distribution and obeys its own LLN and CLT (the median has an asymptotic
normal distribution under mild conditions). The lesson generalizes: the LLN and CLT are about
the *sample mean as an estimator of the population mean*; if the population mean does not exist
or is not what you care about, you change the estimator, not the theorem.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me a concrete distribution where the CLT fails, and say precisely why."* The cleanest
answers, in increasing severity: **(1)** a Pareto with $1<\alpha\le 2$ — the mean exists so
the LLN holds, but the variance is infinite, so the CLT's hypothesis fails and the
$\sqrt n$-scaled mean converges to a *stable law*, not a Gaussian. **(2)** the **Cauchy**
distribution — even the mean is undefined, so the LLN fails too and $\bar X_n$ stays Cauchy
forever; the right estimator is the median. The principle to state out loud: *the CLT requires
finite variance; the LLN (in its usual forms) requires at least a finite mean. Heavy tails
break these in order — first the variance, then the mean — and each break costs you a theorem.*
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-A1 *The Estimator as a Random Variable*]**, which defined the
sampling distribution and the standard error and left two questions open. This article
answered them: the **LLN** says the sample mean converges to the truth (the standard error
shrinks to $0$), and the **CLT** says the standardized error is asymptotically Gaussian (the
standard error becomes a usable margin of error). From here:

- **[INFR-B4 *Why Maximum Likelihood Works*]** reuses this exact machinery: the asymptotic
  normality of the MLE, $\sqrt n(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0, I^{-1})$, is
  proved by Taylor-expanding the score and applying the **CLT** to a sum of i.i.d. score
  terms — the same Step-2-through-Step-6 move done here, with the score in place of $X_i$.
- **[INFR-C1 *Confidence Intervals*]** builds the interval previewed in @eq-ci-preview into a
  careful object, and pins down what "$95\%$ confident" means (it is a property of the
  procedure, licensed by the CLT's coverage guarantee).
- **[INFR-C2 *The t-Distribution*]** handles the small-$n$, unknown-$\sigma$ correction that
  Slutsky waved away asymptotically here.
- **[INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*]** gets its null distributions — the $z$, $t$, $\chi^2$,
  and $F$ reference curves — from CLT-type limits of test statistics.
- For heavy-tailed and small-sample cases where the CLT is untrustworthy (@sec-heavy,
  @sec-cauchy), **[INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap*]** is the assumption-light alternative — it
  simulates the sampling distribution instead of assuming it is Gaussian.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Jacob Bernoulli, *Ars Conjectandi* (1713), for the original LLN
  ("golden theorem"); Abraham de Moivre, *The Doctrine of Chances* (1738 ed.), for the first
  central-limit result on coin flips; and Lindeberg's 1922 and Lyapunov's 1901 papers for the
  general finite-variance CLT via characteristic functions.
- **The honest-rate result.** The **Berry–Esseen theorem** (@sec-n30) is the quantitative
  refinement: it converts "$Z_n$ is approximately normal" into an explicit $1/\sqrt n$ error
  bound scaled by the third moment. It is the right citation whenever someone invokes
  "$n\ge 30$."
- **Beyond i.i.d.** The CLT holds far beyond identical distributions — the **Lindeberg** and
  **Lyapunov** conditions give CLTs for independent-but-not-identical summands, and there are
  CLTs for *dependent* data (martingale and mixing CLTs) that underpin time-series and MCMC
  inference. The thread to pull is: *what is the weakest condition under which sums become
  Gaussian?*
- **When the limit is not Gaussian.** The Pareto and Cauchy failures (@sec-heavy,
  @sec-cauchy) are not pathologies but a doorway to **stable distributions** and the
  **generalized central limit theorem**: under infinite variance, normalized sums converge to
  non-Gaussian stable laws, with the Gaussian as the special finite-variance case. This is the
  mathematics behind heavy-tailed finance and network models.
- **Open thread this article deferred.** *How fast, for my specific distribution, and how do I
  know my $n$ is big enough?* — answered partly by Berry–Esseen, but in practice by
  resampling, which is the entire point of **[INFR-E1]**.
